name: Plasma Cosmology
description: Plasma Cosmology is a non-standard cosmology that challenges the Big Bang model. It proposes that the universe is primarily composed of plasma, an electrically conductive state of matter, and that electromagnetic forces play a dominant role in shaping cosmic structures. Developed primarily by Hannes Alfvén and later championed by others, it suggests that the universe is infinitely large, without a beginning or end in time, and that large-scale structures like galaxies and filaments are formed by electromagnetic forces between cosmic plasma filaments. Proponents argue that observations like the cosmic microwave background (CMB), galaxy formation, and redshift can be explained by plasma phenomena without invoking dark matter or dark energy. They point to laboratory experiments and simulations demonstrating the formation of filamentary structures in plasma as evidence supporting their claims. Mainstream cosmology views Plasma Cosmology as a fringe theory. The CMB is interpreted as afterglow of the Big Bang, and the success of the Lambda-CDM model, which incorporates dark matter and dark energy, in explaining a wide range of cosmological observations is seen as strong evidence against Plasma Cosmology. Critics argue that Plasma Cosmology lacks a comprehensive mathematical framework, fails to adequately explain the observed abundance of light elements, and relies on selective interpretation of observational data. Furthermore, it struggles to account for the accelerating expansion of the universe without introducing ad-hoc adjustments. While some aspects of plasma physics are undoubtedly important in astrophysics, the claim that electromagnetic forces dominate over gravity on cosmological scales is not supported by the majority of the scientific community. The theory remains a minority viewpoint within astrophysics and cosmology.
subtitle: An alternative to the Big Bang.

claims.claim: Electromagnetic forces dominate gravity on cosmological scales.
claims.evidence: Laboratory experiments showing plasma filament formation; simulations of cosmic structures forming from plasma filaments.
claims.criticism: Gravity is the dominant force at large scales; simulations require unrealistic plasma parameters; electromagnetic forces are screened over large distances.

claims.claim: The universe is infinitely large and eternal, without a Big Bang.
claims.evidence: Observed filamentary structures in the universe resembling plasma formations; redshift explained by plasma effects rather than expansion.
claims.criticism: CMB strongly supports the Big Bang; observed abundance of light elements matches Big Bang nucleosynthesis; redshift is primarily due to the expansion of the universe.

claims.claim: Dark matter and dark energy are unnecessary to explain cosmological observations.
claims.evidence: Plasma effects can account for galaxy rotation curves and accelerated expansion without invoking dark matter or dark energy.
claims.criticism: Dark matter explains galaxy rotation curves and gravitational lensing better than plasma models; dark energy explains the accelerating expansion and is supported by multiple lines of evidence.

keyFigures.name: Hannes Alfvén
keyFigures.role: Pioneer of magnetohydrodynamics and early proponent of Plasma Cosmology

keyFigures.name: Anthony Peratt
keyFigures.role: Leading researcher and advocate of Plasma Cosmology

keyFigures.name: Eric Lerner
keyFigures.role: Author and popularizer of Plasma Cosmology

keyFigures.name: George Field
keyFigures.role: Astrophysicist; critic of Plasma Cosmology and proponent of the standard cosmological model

mythologyConnections: Creation myths involving light/energy (plasma as a fundamental energy source)

timeline.event: Alfvén begins applying magnetohydrodynamics to cosmology.
Anthony Peratt conducts plasma experiments and simulations supporting Plasma Cosmology.
Eric Lerner publishes 'The Big Bang Never Happened'.
